name: Cat Sith
description: The Cat Sìth (Scottish Gaelic: Cat Sith) is a fairy cat from Scottish folklore. Legend describes it as a black cat with a white spot on its chest, though variations in color exist. It is believed to haunt the Scottish Highlands. Some tales depict the Cat Sìth as a shapeshifter, able to transform into a human nine times before being unable to change back. Other stories suggest it is a witch who can transform into a cat nine times. The Cat Sìth is often associated with Samhain (Halloween), where it is said that people would leave out saucers of milk for it. It was also believed that singing and playing music would keep the Cat Sìth away from their homes. These spectral cats were not always malevolent, but were often mischievous and unpredictable. In some areas, it was thought that the Cat Sìth could steal a person's soul before it could pass on to the afterlife if a proper vigil was not kept. Superstitions surrounding the Cat Sìth highlight the complex relationship between humans and the supernatural in Scottish folklore.
subtitle: Fairy Cat of the Scottish Highlands
appearance: Typically described as a large, black cat with a single white spot on its chest. Some accounts describe it as having glowing eyes and being the size of a large dog.
habitat: The Scottish Highlands, particularly wild and remote areas. Also said to inhabit forests and abandoned buildings.
behavior: The Cat Sìth is generally considered to be mischievous and unpredictable. It may be benevolent or malevolent, depending on the circumstances and the individual cat. It is drawn to music and feasts, but also associated with death and the afterlife.

keyMyths.title: Samhain Offerings
keyMyths.description: On Samhain, it was customary to leave out saucers of milk for the Cat Sìth to appease it and ensure good fortune for the coming year. Failure to do so could result in bad luck or misfortune.

keyMyths.title: The Vigil
keyMyths.description: A vigil was kept over the dead to prevent the Cat Sìth from stealing the soul of the recently deceased. Music and games were played to distract the creature.

sources.author: W. Y. Evans Wentz
sources.description: A comprehensive study of Celtic folklore and fairy beliefs.
sources.text: A comprehensive study of Celtic folklore and fairy beliefs.
